data.title: Mortgage rates continue to inch up, supply remains an issue
data.description:
Mortgage rates continued to climb up, as strength of the economy put upward pressure, while lack of entry-level supply remained an issue, the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey showed.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.93% as of January 9, up from 6.91% last week and 6.66% in the year-ago period.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.14%, compared to 6.13% a week ago and 5.87% a year ago.

"In the first full week of the new year, the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage remained elevated at just under 7 percent,” Sam Khater, Freddie Mac’s Chief Economist, said.

“The continued strength of the economy has put upward pressure on mortgage rates, and along with high home prices, continues to impact housing affordability. The lack of entry-level supply also remains an issue, especially for those looking to become first-time homeowners,” Khater added.

data.title: Globus Medical upgraded to neutral by BoA on Q4 performance
data.description:
BofA Securities has upgraded Globus Medical (NYSE:GMED) to neutral from underperform, citing the company's strong Q4 revenue performance.

The investment bank noted that Globus's (NYSE:GMED) preannounced Q4 revenue was 3% above Street expectations. The company also introduced 2025 revenue and EPS forecasts that were both approximately 1% above Street midpoints.

BofA said it now sees "less integration risk" following the Q4 preannouncement and that "any potential disruption for 2025 is contemplated in the guidance."

"The guide implies topline acceleration which we see as a sign of management's confidence it can deliver on high cross-selling expectations," BofA added.

The bank raised its price target for the stock to $97 from $80.

data.title: UBS poised to pay hundreds of millions over Credit Suisse tax case - report
data.description:
UBS Group (NYSE:UBS) is close to paying hundreds of millions of dollars for alleged violations by Credit Suisse of an earlier agreement with the U.S. Justice Department regarding U.S. clients who evaded tax, according to a media report.

A settlement is close and could be announced as soon as this week, the Wall Street Journal reported on Thursday, citing people familiar with the matter.

In 2014, Credit Suisse pleaded guilty to helping thousands of Americans cheat on their taxes. As part of a plea agreement, the bank paid $2.6B and was required to find and shut down any remaining undeclared American-held accounts.

UBS (NYSE:UBS) acquired Credit Suisse in 2023 after it lost the trust of its customers and investors after years of scandals and billions of dollars paid in penalties.

The WSJ previously reported that the Justice Department investigated Credit Suisse about accounts, containing hundreds of millions of dollars, belonging to U.S. citizens that weren't immediately reported. That lag allowed some U.S. taxpayers to move their funds to other places undetected, the report said.

UBS (UBS) stock rose 3.0% in Thursday trading on the Swiss stock exchange.

data.title: Walgreens’ potential sale to Sycamore Partners in focus during Q1 earnings
data.description:
Walgreens Boots Alliance (NASDAQ:WBA) is set to announce the first quarter earnings on Friday, and investors will keep an eye on updates regarding reports of potential sale to Sycamore Partners.

Wall Street expects the drugstore operator to post EPS of $0.36, indicating a 42.4% Y/Y decline, while revenue is expected to increase 1.9% Y/Y to $37.42 billion during the quarter.

Despite beating sales expectations in Q4, the company is facing headwinds due to ongoing execution challenges, footprint optimization and consumer weakness.

“Additional WBA headwinds could be driven by the weaker flu season to date (y/y), with outpatient visits for influenza like illness tracking below last year's levels,” noted Morgan Stanley analyst Erin Wright.

Seeking Alpha's Quant Ratings, Wall Street and Seeking Alpha analysts are cautious about the company and recommended it as a Hold.

Seeking Alpha analyst Daniel Schönberger said despite the low stock price suggesting undervaluation, Walgreens' profitability struggles and potential dividend cuts make it a risky investment. Additionally, the company’s sustainability is questionable due to negative free cash flow and significant debt levels.

Over the last two years, WBA has beaten EPS estimates 63% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 100% of the time.

Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen one upward revision versus nine downward moves. Revenue estimates have seen four upward revisions against three downward moves.

Wallgreens was one of the worst performing S&P 500 stocks in 2024 as its shares declined nearly 65%.

data.title: Vir Biotech upgraded at Morgan Stanley on cancer drug data
data.description:
Morgan Stanley upgraded Vir Biotechnology (NASDAQ:VIR) to Overweight from Equal Weight on Thursday after the cancer drug developer shared initial mid-stage trial data for two antitumor agents developed using Sanofi’s (SNY) PRO-XTEN masking platform.

Citing initial results from two Phase 2 trials for its experimental therapies, VIR-5818 and VIR-5500, the San Francisco, California-based biotech announced Wednesday that the two T-cell engagers led to early clinical signals with an encouraging safety profile in solid tumors.

The dose escalations for both programs are ongoing, and the treatments targeted at HER2-positive tumors and metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancers haven’t yet indicated a maximum tolerated dose, the company added.

“We believe initial data provide early platform de-risking and see potential for activity to improve as dose escalation continues,” MS analyst Michael Ulz wrote, adding, “We look to data from higher doses to improve on efficacy while confirming a tolerable safety profile.”

The analyst raised his price target on VIR to $20 from $10 per share, taking into account, among other things, potential revenues from VIR-5500 in prostate cancer and the company’s newly released cash guidance of roughly $1.1B as of Jan. 1.

data.title: Drugs not selected for Medicare price negotiation nearly doubled - report
data.description:
The list prices of the top 25 drugs with the highest Medicare Part D spend that are not subject to drug price negotiations have increased by an average 98% since they hit the market, according to a new report.

The AARP analysis also found that more than 40% of the current list prices for these medications is due to price increases that happened after the drugs were first marketed.

The AARP report focused on the 25 drugs with the highest Medicare Part D spend in 2022 whose prices aren't being negotiated. Overall, Medicare spent about $50B on these drugs that year.

The top five drugs on the list are Eli Lilly's (NYSE:LLY) diabetes drug Trulicity (dulaglutide), Novo Nordisk's (NVO) diabetes med Ozempic (semaglutide), GSK's (NYSE:GSK) COPD and asthma treatment Ellepta Trelegy (fluticasone furoate, umeclidinium, and vilanterol inhalation powder), Gilead Sciences' (GILD) HIV med Biktarvy (bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir), and Astellas Pharma (OTCPK:ALPMF)(OTCPK:ALPMY) and Pfizer's (NYSE:PFE) prostate cancer therapy Xtandi (enzalutamide).

Leigh Purvis, a prescription drug policy principal with the AARP Public Policy Institute, noted that the median price of a new brand drug is around $300K annually.

The Inflation Reduction Act, passed in 2022, not only gave the CMS the authority to negotiate drug prices for the first time, but also mandated penalties for drugmakers if their medicines increased in price faster than inflation.

Cheaper, negotiated prices for the first 10 drugs selected by CMS will go into effect on Jan. 1, 2026, while up to an additional 15 drugs will be selected for negotiation by Feb. 1, 2025.

data.title: TMT outshone most sectors in 2024, here is how well it performed
data.description:
The technology, media and telecommunications sector was among the best performing ones last year.

Along with consumer discretionary (XLY) and financials (XLF), its returns were less dependent on multiple expansion than gains elsewhere in the index due to earnings upside, said Venu Krishna, head of U.S. Equity Research at Barclays, in a note.

However, financials and technology were trading almost as expensive as they have ever been over the past decade.

“TMT kicked off 2024 with strong returns that weakened as we entered the back half, while discretionary (XLY) performance improved from the second quarter onward,” said Krishna. “The fourth quarter weakness was attributable primarily to materials (XLB) and healthcare (XLV).”

  • Communication services (XLC) was up 15.6%, 9.1%, 1.4%, and 14.1% respectively each quarter. The sector was up 38.9% in 2024.
  • Information technology (XLK) was up 12.5%, 13.6%, 1.4%, and 4.7%, respectively each quarter. The sector was up 35.7% in 2024.

Also, TMT’s 2024 outperformance was due no doubt to Big Tech’s outsized returns, driven by earnings revisions outshining the rest of the technology sector and the rest of the large-cap stocks.

In addition, TMT and the consumer sectors (XLY), (XLP) were responsible for nearly 60% of S&P 500 Index’s (SPX) outperformance, excluding Big Tech, against their European counterparts in 2024. Barclays analysts expect this upside to continue in 2025 “as long as the U.S. consumer remains healthy.”

Technology, media and telecom ETFs: (NYSEARCA:XHYT), (SOCL), (GGME), (XTL), (IYZ), (VGT), (IYW), (XLK).

data.title: Sanofi reports positive results for subcutaneous Sarclisa
data.description:
Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY) said that a Phase 3 study of its new subcutaneous formulation of Sarclisa in the treatment of multiple myeloma met its co-primary endpoints, demonstrating non-inferiority to the intravenous formulation.

The study, called IRAKLIA, showed that Sarclisa administered subcutaneously via an on-body delivery system, or OBDS, in combination with pomalidomide and dexamethasone, or Pd, met its co-primary endpoints of non-inferior objective response rate and observed concentration before dosing at steady state compared to intravenous Sarclisa administered at a weight-based dose in combination with Pd in patients with relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma, according to a statement.

Sanofi added that key secondary endpoints were also achieved. The study was conducted using Enable Injections’ enFuse hands-free OBDS.

Full results from the study will be presented at a future medical meeting.

data.title: Bank of Montreal, Canadian Imperial Bank upgraded to Outperform at RBC
data.description:
RBC Capital Markets upgraded Bank of Montreal (NYSE:BMO) and Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce (NYSE:CM) to Outperform from Sector Perform in a recent note, as analyst Darko Mihelic sees a "transitional year" for Canadian lenders in 2025.

The banks within Mihelic's coverage, including (NYSE:BMO) and (NYSE:CM), are expected to boost their share buyback activity in the second half of 2025, potentially paving the way for even larger repurchases in the following year.

Mihelic wrote in a note to clients that while the outlook for this year is relatively positive, "we see several reasons for a more favorable landscape for the group in 2026."

These key drivers include "a stabilizing Canadian economy, reduced concerns on mortgage payment shock (although mortgage volumes may increase), peaking credit losses, stronger capital markets and wealth revenue growth, stabilizing capital, and buyback activity which is restarting," he explained.

Seeking Alpha's peer comparison page displays various metrics of BMO and CM.

data.title: Shein pushing for April London IPO
data.description:
Fast fashion retailer Shein could complete their £50B ($62B) London IPO as early as April, according to sources cited by Reuters, enabling FTSE-100-listed Shein shares to be incorporated into the pension portfolios of millions of UK citizens.

While the Singapore-based company pivoted from a U.S. IPO to one in London seven months ago to avoid the more rigid regulatory hurdles and political pressure in the U.S., Shein still faces challenges pertaining to concerns that the company uses slave labor drawn from the Uyghur population and sources cotton from the banned Xinjiang region of China.

The British parliament recently summoned representatives from Shein and Temu (NASDAQ:PDD) to answer questions on its labor practices, and use of banned Xinjiang cotton, but were frustrated by the company’s evasiveness. The lack of full disclosure could make a London IPO just as problematic as the one the company faced in the U.S.

“The reluctance to answer basic questions has frankly bordered on contempt of the committee,” UK committee chair for business and trade Liam Byrne was quoted by The Guardian as saying in response to Shein’s unwillingness to answer questions about its manufacturing processes.

Shein, however, hopes that an upcoming meeting between Britain’s finance minister Rachel Reeves with Chinese vice premier He Lifeng will grease the troublesome UK regulatory wheels and allow for a springtime Shein IPO.
